content.claim: School closure/retention decisions carry distributional consequences for ethnic and socioeconomic groups: village segregation via 'white flight' of non-Roma pupils to other schools is documented, Roma children constitute 45% of students in segregated schools or classrooms nationally, and consolidation/closure removes local options most accessible to poorer and Roma families with lower mobility.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Education finance lens
limitations: Figures on segregation are national-level, not specific to rural closure decisions
Causal attribution of segregation to consolidation policy itself not directly demonstrated
Data on whether closures specifically accelerate or retard segregation trends not provided
Teacher incentive misalignment (job security vs. student welfare) documented as mechanism but impact magnitude unknown
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
